title: Muslim Family Law, Prenuptial Agreements, and the Emergence of Dowry in Bangladesh<sup>*</sup>
abstract: We explain trends in dowry levels in Bangladesh by drawing attention to an institutional feature of marriage contracts previously ignored in the literature: mehr or traditional Islamic bride-price. We develop a model of marriage contracts in which mehr serves as a barrier to husbands exiting marriage and a component of dowry as an amount that ex ante compensates the groom for the cost of mehr. We investigate how mehr and dowry respond to exogenous changes in the costs of polygamy and divorce, and show that our model gives a different set of predictions than traditional models. We show that major changes in dowry levels took place precisely after the legal changes, corresponding to simultaneous changes in levels of mehr.
summary_en: Bangladesh has seen dowry (payments from bride's family to groom) rise sharply. Why? Earlier explanations pointed to marriage-market imbalances. This paper offers a different mechanism: mehr, the traditional Islamic bride-price that a husband is obliged to pay if he divorces his wife. Dowry emerges partly as ex-ante compensation to the groom for the mehr obligation. The model predicts specific responses of dowry and mehr to changes in divorce and polygamy laws — and the authors show that observed dowry shifts in Bangladesh tracked such legal changes closely. A clean example of how customs adapt to legal reform.
data_used: Marriage-contract and dowry data from Bangladesh across periods of legal reform; mehr and dowry levels tracked against changes in divorce and polygamy legislation.
policy_relevance: Limited direct relevance to Hungarian policy. Relevant to CEU/KRTK research on marriage, family law, and customary institutions in developing countries. Offers a methodological template for how legal reforms ripple through informal marriage institutions. Caveat: this is a case study of a specific legal-cultural context; policy implications are for development settings rather than Hungary.
